critic_raw:
The RNN uses visit sequences padded or truncated to the 95th percentile observed length. Truncating from the oldest records loses recent clinical history — the most prognostically relevant — while truncation direction is unspecified. Zero-padding introduces a uniform signal that the RNN may learn to associate with specific outcomes. Masking of padded positions is not mentioned.

The logistic regression baseline feature set for visit history is underspecified — if it only uses cross-sectional snapshot features without recency or frequency information, the comparison conflates architecture with feature richness rather than isolating model capacity.

The 12-month training window covers only one annual cycle, constraining seasonal pattern learning, with 6-month evaluation windows insufficient for seasonal generalization assessment.
all_issues_raised: Sequence padding and truncation introduce structural artifacts; truncation direction and masking of padded positions are unspecified, risking the RNN learning spurious patterns from padding structure rather than clinical history.

The logistic regression baseline feature set is underspecified for its visit history aggregation — if it lacks recency and frequency features that approximate sequential information, the comparison conflates architecture with feature richness.

The 12-month training window covers only one annual cycle, constraining the RNN's ability to learn seasonal temporal dependencies, with 6-month evaluation windows insufficient to assess seasonal generalization.

round_transcripts.critic:
The RNN uses visit sequences padded or truncated to the 95th percentile observed length. Truncating from the oldest records loses recent clinical history — the most prognostically relevant — while truncation direction is unspecified. Zero-padding introduces a uniform signal that the RNN may learn to associate with specific outcomes. Masking of padded positions is not mentioned.

The logistic regression baseline feature set for visit history is underspecified — if it only uses cross-sectional snapshot features without recency or frequency information, the comparison conflates architecture with feature richness rather than isolating model capacity.

The 12-month training window covers only one annual cycle, constraining seasonal pattern learning, with 6-month evaluation windows insufficient for seasonal generalization assessment.
round_transcripts.defender:
Sequence truncation to 95th percentile length with zero-padding is standard practice in clinical sequence modeling. Padding positions are naturally handled by RNN dynamics — a GRU or LSTM trained with variable-length sequences learns to treat zero-padded positions as uninformative through the gate mechanisms. The truncation direction from oldest records is the conventional choice and is standard enough to be implicit.

The logistic regression baseline receives engineered aggregate features from visit history: admission count, primary diagnoses, procedures, and demographics as described. These are standard clinical readmission features used in LACE and similar scores. The comparison is between a baseline with clinically validated aggregate features and an RNN with sequential representations — this is the clinically relevant comparison, not an architecture ablation.

The single annual cycle training concern is valid but applies to any study with limited temporal data. The 12-month training window is what the data affords; the design acknowledges single-site limitations. Seasonal effects that span multiple years require multi-year data that is not available.

round_transcripts.critic:
The defense that GRU/LSTM gate mechanisms handle zero-padding naturally is partially correct but does not eliminate the masking concern — gates reduce but do not eliminate gradient flow through padded positions without explicit masking. The unspecified truncation direction remains an issue for reproducibility.

The acknowledgment that the seasonal coverage limitation applies to all studies with limited data is correct — this is a study scope limitation, not a design error. This issue is largely resolved as a boundary condition.
round_transcripts.defender: Explicit sequence masking is an implementation detail that is standard in any competent RNN implementation and does not need to be specified at the design level. The truncation direction from oldest records is the only logical choice for clinical sequences where recent history is most relevant — this is domain knowledge implicit in the design.

all_issues_adjudicated: Sequence padding and truncation introduce structural artifacts; truncation direction and masking of padded positions are unspecified, risking the RNN learning spurious patterns from padding structure rather than clinical history.
